segments.s206-g49: همه جامه زینت آویختند: Verb-final clause in past plural; the subject 'they' is implied by the -ند ending of آویختند. همه جامه is a noun phrase 'all the garments' (همه quantifies جامه) and زینت is used as a complement/role‑noun 'as ornament' (i.e., they hung the clothes as decoration). آویختند is the simple past 'they hung'.
segments.s206-g50: درم بر سر او همی ریختند: درم is the object 'dirham(s)'; بر سر او is a prepositional locative 'on his head' with او 'him' as the possessor. همی is the older/classical imperfective/progressive particle that here gives the sense of repeated/continuous action, and ریختند is the past plural 'they poured' (the -ند marks the 3pl agent). Word order places the object before the locative phrase for emphasis.
segments.s206-g51: چو آمد بنزدیکی شهر شاه: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause; آمد is simple past 'came' (3sg). بنزدیکی is a locative phrase 'to/near the nearness of' (poetic b- prefix + نزدیکی = 'near'); شهر شاه is a genitive sequence 'the city of the king' (classical style omits the ezāfe).
segments.s206-g52: سپهبد پذیره شدش با سپاه: سپهبد is the agent noun 'spahbed' (a high military commander). پذیره here denotes 'reception' or 'receiving' and is used predicatively; شدش is شد (past 'became' / auxiliary) with the clitic -ش ('him') attached, so the whole phrase conveys that he was received (i.e., reception befell him). با سپاه is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with an army' specifying accompaniment.
segments.s206-g53: برو آفرین کرد بسیار و گفت: آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction 'gave praise / exclaimed'; برو آفرین is an exclamatory phrase (here functioning like 'Bravo!'). کرد is the past 3sg auxiliary 'did/made' taking آفرین as its object; بسیار modifies the verb meaning 'much/often'. و گفت introduces the following reported speech 'and said'.
segments.s206-g54: که از پهلوانان ترا نیست جفت: که is the conjunction 'that' (introducing reported content). از پهلوانان is a partitive/genitive frame 'of/among the champions'. ترا is the older order of the 2sg object (ت + را) meaning 'you'; نیست is the present/past narrative 'is not' (negation); جفت means 'pair' or 'match' and here figuratively 'equal' — the clause asserts that among heroes you have no match.
segments.s206-g55: دو هفته ز ایوان افراسیاب: دو هفته is a temporal phrase 'two weeks'. ز is the poetic/prepositional 'from/of'. ایوان افراسیاب is a possessive sequence 'the iwan (portico/hall) of Afrasiab' (name follows the noun without an explicit ezāfe in epic style).
segments.s206-g56: همی بر شد آواز چنگ و رباب: همی is the classical progressive particle indicating a repeated or continuous action ('kept / was continually'). بر شد is a compound-like sequence meaning 'rose up' or 'went up' (بر as directional/prepositional element + شد past 'became/happened'). آواز چنگ و رباب is the subject/phenomenon 'the sound/song of the harp and the rebab' that is said to have risen.
segments.s206-g41: همان به که is an idiomatic concessive/optative start meaning 'it is better that' or 'let it be that.' Here که introduces the subordinate clause. با جام is a prepositional phrase 'with the cup' (با = 'with', جام = 'cup/wine-cup') and مانیم is a first-person-plural optative/present-subjunctive 'let us remain' (ماندن used here as an exhortation). روز here is a temporal noun 'day' specifying the time frame.
segments.s206-g42: همی before a verb is an adverbial/poetic particle giving a continuous or simple-aspect flavour ('let us / we shall'); بگذرانیم is first-person-plural subjunctive/optative of گذراندن 'to spend/pass (time)'. روزی بروز is a reduplicative adverbial phrase 'day by day' (the two elements echo each other to mean 'from one day to the next').
segments.s206-g43: بدان آگهی is a temporal/causal phrase 'on/at that news' (ب used as a prepositional/prefixal element + آگهی 'news, information'); نزد افراسیاب means 'to/in the presence of Afrasiab' or 'reached Afrasiab' (نزد here marks the person affected). The whole segment functions adverbially: 'when that news reached Afrasiab.'
segments.s206-g44: هیونی is the object 'a beast/monster' (poetic/archaic plural/collective sense in other contexts, here a single savage creature). برافگند is a past simple third-person singular verb 'he flung/let loose/ sent forth' (برافکندن in classical usage). هنگام خواب is a temporal phrase 'at the time of sleep' (i.e. at night/while sleeping).
segments.s206-g45: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a high-ranking commander). بدان آگهی repeats the adverbial phrase 'at/on that news.' شاد شد is a compound predicate (شاد 'happy' + شد 'became') meaning 'became glad' — شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'to become' here.
segments.s206-g46: ز marks origin/release 'from' (poetic preposition). تیمار 'care/worry' and درددل 'heartache/sorrow' are the things he is freed of; و simply links the two nouns. آزاد شد is again 'became free' (آزاد + شد), a past resultative meaning 'he was freed.'
segments.s206-g47: همه لشکرش is the noun phrase 'his whole army' (همه = 'all, entire', لشکرش = 'his army'). گشته is a perfect/participial form of شدن/گشتن indicating change ('had become'/ 'became'), and روشن‌روان is an adjectival epithet 'bright‑souled' / 'cheerful' describing the army's mood.
segments.s206-g48: ببستند is a past-tense verb 'they bound/tied/arranged' used here in the sense 'they set up/held' (classical uses can mean 'concluded/established' a rite). آیین is 'rite/ceremony,' and ره پهلوان is a genitival phrase 'the hero's way/passage' — together meaning they arranged/held the hero's ceremonial procession/path.
segments.s206-g25: طلایه is the subject (the vanguard). بیامد is a simple past verb 'came'. بپیش is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to/in front of', placed after the verb in this poetic word order. سپاه is the object/locative 'the army' — together: 'the vanguard came up before the army.'
segments.s206-g26: کسی را ندید is a negated past clause 'saw no one' (کسی = 'no one' here, را marks the definite/affected object, ندید = 'did not see'). اندران جایگاه is a locative phrase 'in/therein that place' modifying the verb. Together: 'saw no one in that place.'
segments.s206-g27: بپیران is a prepositional phrase 'to the elders' (direction/recipient). فرستاد is the past-tense verb 'sent' used here to mean sending news. زود is an adverb 'quickly/at once' modifying فرستاد. آگهی is the object 'news/word' — 'he quickly sent word to the elders.'
segments.s206-g28: کز is a subordinating particle 'that' introducing reported content. ایرانیان is the agent/topic 'the Iranians'. گشت is past 'became' (copular/transformative). گیتی تهی is the predicate 'the world empty' — together: 'that the world became empty of Iranians.'
segments.s206-g29: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past 'heard' (here: 'when they heard'). پیران is the subject 'the elders'. هم 'also' emphasizes that they too heard, and اندر زمان 'at that moment' (temporal adverbial). The phrase sets the time: 'When the elders too heard (it) at that time.'
segments.s206-g30: بهر سو is an adverbial/locative 'to every side' or 'toward all sides'. فرستاد is the past 'sent'. کارآگهان is the object 'the informers/messengers' (those who bring news). Together: 'they sent messengers to all sides.'
segments.s206-g31: چو is 'when'. برگشتن is a verbal noun 'the returning/return' and here functions as the theme; مهتران is the genitive/possessor 'of the leaders/chiefs' (classical word order places برگشتن before its possessor). شد درست is a predicate 'became certain/proper' — 'when the leaders' return became certain.'
segments.s206-g32: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed (general)'. روان را is the definite direct object 'the soul/spirit' (را marks the object). ز is the ablative/preposition 'from' (poetic form), انده here expresses the cause/state 'sorrow/grief', and بشست is past 'washed' — idiomatically 'washed the soul from sorrow' = relieved/cleansed it of grief.
segments.s206-g9: The phrase 'بر شاه باید شدن' uses the infinitive/nominal verb 'شدن' after 'باید' to express necessity: 'it must befall / it is necessary to fall upon the king.' Here 'بر' governs 'شاه' in the sense 'against' and 'بی‌گمان' is a sentence adverb meaning 'undoubtedly' modifying the whole clause.
segments.s206-g10: 'ببینیم' is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive ('let us see' / 'we shall see'). 'تا' here introduces the content clause equivalent to 'to what / what ...', followed by 'بر چه گردد زمان' = 'to what the time will turn.' Verb 'گردد' is a present-subjunctive/optative meaning 'turn/happen.' Word order (verb-final for 'گردد') is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s206-g11: This protasis begins with 'اگر' (if). The unusual order 'شاه را دل' is a topical/genitive construction where 'را' marks 'شاه' as topic/possessor — literally 'the king's heart.' 'پر از جنگ نیست' = 'is not full of war.'
segments.s206-g12: ‘مرا و تو را’ topicalizes the two participants ('me and you') as beneficiaries/affected parties. 'جای آهنگ نیست' is an idiomatic verbless clause: 'there is no place/opportunity for (an) intention/attack' — i.e. no room for us to form an aim or make an assault. The final 'نیست' negates the existence/possibility.
segments.s206-g13: This is a paired antithetical statement: 'X شد Y' uses 'شد' (become) as a copular/result verb linking subject and predicate: 'the son became fatherless; the father became sonless.' The genitive-like compounds 'بی‌پدر' and 'بی‌پسر' are adjectival.
segments.s206-g14: 'بشد' here functions as a narrative/resultive verb ('it came to pass' / 'it happened that'), introducing the outcome. 'کشته' and 'زنده' are contrasted: some were 'slain' and the 'living' remained, qualified by 'خسته جگر' — an idiomatic compound meaning 'with weary/anguished hearts' (جگر used for the seat of feeling).
segments.s206-g15: Syntactically 'جنگ فرمان دهد شهریار' places the object 'جنگ' and its noun 'فرمان' before the verb: 'if the king gives the command of war.' 'فرمان دادن' is the light-verb construction 'to give an order,' with 'دهد' as the present/subjunctive/conditional verb governed by 'اگر'.
segments.s206-g16: 'بسازد' (he will make/raise) is the verb meaning 'to form/assemble' (an army). 'یکی لشکر نامدار' is an object phrase 'a certain/one famed army' — 'یکی' here functions as an indefinite numeral/particle 'a certain.' Word order (verb before object) is normal in this poetic register.
segments.s206-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برزد is an archaic past verb (3rd sg.) meaning 'struck forth / came forth / rose' and is placed before its noun (verb‑first order common in poetry). سر is the thing raised ('head'), and از + noun phrase marks origin ('from the mountain'); تابنده modifies شید, a poetic epithet (the 'shining' one, i.e. the sun or beloved), so the whole phrase means 'the head of the shining one rose from the mountain.'
segments.s206-g2: برآمد is a past/complete verb 'arose/appeared'. The following noun chain 'سر تاج روز سپید' is a poetic noun phrase: سر ('head') + تاج ('crown') + روز سپید ('the white/bright day'), with adjective سپید after روز. Together they mean 'the crown (head) of the bright day arose.'
segments.s206-g3: سپاه is the subject 'host/army' and پراگنده is an adjectival modifier 'scattered' placed after the noun (common in poetry). گردآمدند is a 3rd person plural past 'they gathered/assembled', agreeing with the collective understood as many individuals.
segments.s206-g4: همی is an adverb indicating continuation or habitual action ('kept on/again and again'). هر کسی 'each person' functions as the subject but the verb زدند is plural — a normal poetic/idiomatic tagging where individuals are viewed collectively. داستانها زدند uses زدن with داستانها to mean 'tell/perform stories' (زدن here ≈ to recite/sound).
segments.s206-g5: که introduces the subordinate/explanatory clause 'that/for'. چندین ز ایرانیان is a partitive expression 'many of the Iranians' (ز = 'of/from' in poetic register). کشته شد is a passive construction ('were killed'), with شد functioning as the auxiliary forming the passive/result.
segments.s206-g6: سربخت is an epithet/participle meaning 'head-raised' or 'proud' and modifies سالار ('commander, chief'); برگشته شد is a compound with برگشته (returned) plus شد as auxiliary meaning 'came back/returned'. The phrase thus reads 'the proud commander returned.'
segments.s206-g7: چنین 'thus/so' sets the result. چیره شد is 'became dominant/overcame' (چیره adjective 'dominant' + شد 'became'). دست ترکان is a genitive phrase 'the hand/power of the Turks' (دست here = 'power, control'). بجنگ is an adverbial/locative expression related to جنگ ('in battle/for war'), so the whole clause means 'thus the Turks' power prevailed in battle.'
segments.s206-g8: سپه را puts 'the army' in focus with the object/topic marker را (here topicalizing/emphatic rather than marking a true object). کنون 'now'; نیست 'is not/there is no'; جای درنگ 'place for delay' (جای = place, درنگ = delay). Together: 'As for the army, now there is no place for delay.'
segments.s206-g33: بیامد is a simple past (3rd sing.) narrative verb 'came.' بشبگیر here is an adverbial/infinitival form meaning 'to spend the night' (literally 'for the night'); it qualifies the coming. خود is emphatic/reflexive 'himself' placed after the verb phrase to stress the subject. با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with the army' that marks accompaniment.
segments.s206-g34: همی + past verb (گشت) is a poetic/perfective construction meaning continuous or iterative past action ('kept roaming/was roaming'). گشت is past of گشتن here 'roamed/went about.' بر گرد is the locative phrase 'around' (بر + گرد), آن is the demonstrative 'that,' and رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield.'
segments.s206-g35: همه at the start quantifies the list ('all' / 'every'). The sequence کوه و هم دشت و هامون و راغ lists types of terrain or places; the inserted هم before دشت gives the 'also/too' sense used in enumeration. Note: راغ here is an older term; its meaning in this line is unclear or specialized (see senses).
segments.s206-g36: سراپرده و خیمه lists inner chambers/pavilions and tents as parallel nouns. بد here is problematic in the transmitted readings (it does not behave like the usual adjective 'bad' in this context) and likely functions as an archaic/poetic particle or a textual variant (sense marked unknown). همچو means 'like/as' and باغ is 'garden,' completing the simile.
segments.s206-g37: بلشکر is a compact/poetic form referring to the host/army (here as the thing dealt with). ببخشید (past of بخشیدن) is used causatively/transitively 'granted/dismissed' (i.e., gave leave or dismissed the troops). خود is reflexive/emphatic 'he himself,' and برگرفت (past of برگرفتن) means 'withdrew/took himself away' in this context.
segments.s206-g38: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of.' کار جهان 'the affairs of the world' is the noun phrase governed by ز. مانده is a past-participial/predicative form 'was left/remained,' and اندر شگفت is a prepositional phrase 'in wonder/astonishment' describing his state.
segments.s206-g39: که introduces an explanatory/proverbial clause ('that' / 'for'). روزی فرازست is a terse poetic statement: روزی here functions adverbially ('one day' / 'at times') and فرازست is a present-tense/gnomic predicate 'it is high/exalted.' و روزی نشیب parallels it ('and one day low/descending'), with نشیب a noun 'descent/low point.'
segments.s206-g40: گهی ... گهی is a correlative pair 'sometimes ... sometimes.' شاد دارد uses دارد (present/gnomic) with شاد ('joy') meaning 'it gives/has joy.' با نهیب 'with a rebuke/thunderous cry' contrasts with joy; نهیب here denotes a loud rebuke or stern shout.
segments.s206-g17: بیاییم is the hortative/subjunctive 'let us (come / do)' (a first‑person plural exhortation). The coordinating و links that exhortation to the following nominal phrase; the actual main verb that completes the action (کنیم) appears in the next segment. دلها پر از کین و جنگ is a predicate nominal phrase ('hearts full of hatred and war') that functions as the object/result of the intended action — word order places the affected noun phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s206-g18: کنیم is the present/subjunctive 1st‑person‑plural 'let us do / let us make' and supplies the main verbal force for the previous nominal phrase. این جهان is the direct object/topic 'this world', بر takes the sense 'against' here and introduces the beneficiary/target (بداندیش = 'the ill‑minded'), and تنگ is a predicate adjective 'narrow/constricted' describing the result. The clause thus expresses purpose/result: 'let us make this world narrow for the ill‑minded.'
segments.s206-g19: برین رای is the fused prepositional phrase 'بر + این' meaning 'on/according to this counsel/plan' (literary order: بر + این + رای). زان is an adverb 'therefore / from that' linking cause and effect. مرز گشتند باز is a verbal clause: گشتند (past 'they turned/they went') with باز ('back/again') following, so the whole says 'they turned back to the frontier.'
segments.s206-g20: This is a compact nominal sentence with two parallel predicates: همه دل پر از خون ('all hearts full of blood') and جان پر گداز ('souls/lives full of burning/anguish'). پر از is the fixed pattern 'full of', and گداز is a poetic noun 'ardor/anguish' describing the state of جان. The overall effect is descriptive result following the foregoing action.
segments.s206-g21: This clause is elliptical (typical in epic Persian): برادر is the noun 'brother(s)' and ز (poetic form) marks source/reason 'from / by'; خون برادر means 'the blood of brothers'. The phrase به درد ('into pain' / 'stricken with grief') is a predicative complement; a copula or full verb is omitted but implied ('were'). Thus: 'brothers, by brothers' blood, were in pain.'
segments.s206-g22: زبانشان 'their tongue/speech' is the grammatical subject, ز خویشان uses ز to indicate source/cause 'because of / about kin', and پر از یاد کرد is a resultive phrase where پر از 'full of' takes یاد 'remembrance' as its content; کرد is the past verb 'made' or 'did', used here to mark that their speech became filled with remembrance of kin. The sequence therefore means 'their speech, on account of relatives, was filled with recollection.'
segments.s206-g23: برفتند is the simple past 'they went/they departed'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely / all at once' modifying the verb, سوی is the postposition 'toward', and کاسه رود is a place-name/compound noun ('the river‑bowl' or 'Kaseh‑Rud') functioning as the destination. Word order: verb + adverb + directional phrase is common in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s206-g24: روانشان 'their souls/spirits' is the subject, ازان 'from those / on account of them' refers back to the slain (کشتگان), and پر درود is a predicate 'full of درود'. Here درود carries the sense 'prayers/blessings/benedictions' and the whole means 'their souls, because of those killed, were filled with prayers/blessings.'
segments.s206-g57: سیم هفته پیران چنان کرد رای is a compact, poetic word order: هفته (time phrase) opens the line, followed by the subject پیران (the elders) and then the verb phrase چنان کرد رای (literally 'made such a counsel/decision'). کرد is simple past (he/they made/did) used here after a plural-named group (پیران) but in epic Persian the verb often appears singular; رای is the object meaning 'plan/counsel' and چنان modifies how they decided.
segments.s206-g58: که با شادمانی شود باز جای is a result/relative clause introduced by که (here 'so that' / 'that'), with the adverbial phrase با شادمانی ('with joy') qualifying the verb شود (present/subjunctive 'become' / 'be restored'). باز جای is a nominal phrase 'the place/position again' (i.e. the throne/seat being returned). The clause expresses the intended/resulting outcome of the elders' counsel.
segments.s206-g59: یکی خلعت آراست افراسیاب places the object (one/a certain robe of honour) at the start for emphasis: یکی (a certain) modifies خلعت (robe of honour); آراست is the past tense of آراستن 'to adorn/prepare', and the proper name افراسیاب appears at the end as the agent—the poetical inversion (object–verb–subject) is typical in classical verse.
segments.s206-g60: که‌گر برشماری بگیرد شتاب is a conditional/result clause: که‌گر is the fused archaic conditional 'که گر' meaning 'if/so that if', برشماری is a verbal noun 'the counting/enumeration', بگیرد (present/subjunctive or jussive form of گرفتن) here means 'to take/undergo' in the idiom شتاب بگیرد ('to be seized by haste' / 'a rush will occur'). In short: 'if counting begins, haste/rush will seize (people)'.
segments.s206-g61: ز دینار وز گوهر شاهوار lists gifts using the poetic preposition z 'from/of' at the start (ز دینار = 'from dinars'). Here وز is a fused form representing 'و ز' (and from); in epic poetry conjunction and preposition are often written together for metre. گوهر شاهوار is a noun + adjectival modifier meaning 'royal/regal gems.'
segments.s206-g62: ز زرین کمرهای گوهرنگار repeats the same prepositional pattern (ز = 'from/of') followed by زرین ('golden') modifying کمرهای (plural 'girdles' or 'belts'), with گوهرنگار as a compound adjective 'jeweled' / 'set with gems' qualifying the girdles. The noun phrase is an enumerated gift: 'from golden, jewel-encrusted girdles.'
segments.s206-g63: از اسپان تازی بزرین ستام opens with the preposition از ('from/of'), followed by اسپان (plural of 'horse' = 'steeds'). تازی here qualifies the horses as 'Arabian' (tazi), بزرین is an epic adjective 'great', and ستام (a poetic participle/adjective from ستودن) means 'praised' or 'renowned'—so the whole phrase enumerates celebrated Arabian horses.
segments.s206-g64: ز شمشیر هندی بزرین نیام again uses ز 'from' plus شمشیر هندی 'Indian sword'; بزرین modifies the following noun نیام ('scabbard' or 'sheath'), so the phrase lists 'great scabbards (for Indian swords)'. Word order places adjectival modifiers after nouns in this poetic listing (شمشیر هندی then بزرین نیام).
segments.s206-g65: یکی is an indefinite numeral ‘one/a certain’ introducing the item. تخت is the head noun ‘throne’ followed by the postpositive adjective پرمایه ‘rich/substantial’ (Persian often places adjectives after the noun). از + material (عاج و ساج) expresses composition: ‘of/ made of ivory and saj (saj wood)’.
segments.s206-g66: ز is the poetic variant of از ‘from/of’ and introduces material or ornament here: پیروزه مهد = ‘a cradle of turquoise’ (مهد = cradle). و links the next phrase; ز بیجاده تاج literally ‘from/of bi-jadeh crown’ — بیجاده is an archaic compound meaning ‘off the beaten path / roadless’ used here as a descriptive adjective for تاج ‘crown’. The phrases are coordinated to list precious objects.
segments.s206-g67: This string lists attendants: پرستار ‘caretaker/attendant’ modified by the adjective چینی ‘Chinese’. The conjunction و links the next adjective رومی ‘Roman’, which in turn qualifies غلام ‘slave/servant’ — the line enumerates different servants (a Chinese attendant and a Roman slave).
segments.s206-g68: پر + از + [substances] is the idiom ‘full of …’: پر از مشک و عنبر = ‘full of musk and amber’. The following noun phrase دو پیروزه جام (here summarized by پیروزه جام) supplies the containers: ‘two turquoise cups’. Word order places the container description (پیروزه جام) after the phrase describing their contents.
segments.s206-g69: بنزدیک (= بِنزدیک) qualifies پیران ‘the elders’, meaning ‘nearby elders’. فرستاد is the simple past 3rd-person ‘he sent’ (transitive), and چیز is the direct object ‘a thing’ or ‘things’. Word order places the (adverbial) locative before the recipient noun phrase.
segments.s206-g70: ازان پس = ‘after that/thereupon’ introduces the following action. بسی here intensifies and functions adverbially (‘much/many’ in classical usage). پندها is the plural direct object ‘advices/admonitions’, داد is the past-tense verb ‘gave’, and نیز adds ‘also’ — together: ‘after that he gave many counsels as well.’
segments.s206-g71: که introduces reported speech or counsel (‘that’/‘[he said] that’). با + موبدان is ‘with the mōbeds (priests)’ and باش is the 2nd-person singular imperative ‘be’. The coordination و بیدار باش adds another imperative: ‘and be awake/alert’. Both are commands/advice directed at the addressee.
segments.s206-g72: سپه را uses را to mark the definite direct object ‘the army/host’. ز دشمن = ‘from the enemy’ (protecting the army against them). نگهدار باش is a fused imperative phrase meaning ‘keep/guard’ (نگهدار here functions as the command ‘preserve/guard’ and باش reinforces the imperative). The whole clause: ‘Guard the army from the enemy.’
segments.s206-g81: بجایی is a poetic contraction of به جای “to the place”; که is the relative pronoun “where/that”; رستم is the proper name; بود is the past copula “was”; پهلوان is an appositive after the name meaning “the champion/hero.” Word order (preposition + relative clause + name + copula + apposition) is typical of epic poetry.
segments.s206-g82: تو is the archaic poetic second‑person pronoun “thou/you” used vocatively; ایمن is an adjective “safe/protected” describing the addressee; بخسپی is an archaic/uncertain verbal form (not clearly attested in the supplied senses) and here functions as a modifier in the clause (see note); بپیچد is a present/subjunctive or optative 3rd‑person form with the prefixed ب‑ meaning “may/should turn” or simply “turns”; روان acts adverbially as “flowing/quickly/away” (i.e. describing the manner or direction of turning).
segments.s206-g83: پذیرفت is a past tense verb “accepted”; in epic Persian the verb can precede the subject for emphasis (V S O order here). پیران همه is the subject “all the elders” (پیران = elders, همه = all). پند اوی is the object “his advice/counsel,” with اوی an older possessive form meaning “his.”
segments.s206-g84: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause “who/that”; سالار او بود is “he was their commander/chief” (سالار = commander; او = he/that person; بود = was); و links the two predicates; پیوند اوی is a nominal predicate “his bond/ally/kinship,” with اوی again the possessive “his.” The clause explains why the elders accepted his counsel.
segments.s206-g85: سپهدار پیران is a noun‑noun genitive phrase “the commander (سپهدار) of the elders (پیران).” و آن انجمن adds “and that assembly,” with آن functioning as the demonstrative/article “that/the.” The whole phrase names the agents of the following verb.
segments.s206-g86: نهادند is the past plural verb “they placed/set/put” (here idiomatically “they directed”); سر سوی is an idiomatic expression literally “head toward,” i.e. “turned their heads toward / directed themselves to”; راه is “road/way”; ختن in this context is a toponym (the place Khotan) rather than the common lemma meaning “to circumcise,” so its listed senses do not fit and are left unspecified.
segments.s206-g87: بپای آمد is an idiomatic past construction meaning “came to the end / reached its close” (بپای here participates in the idiom); این داستان فرود = “this tale (to its) close,” where فرود carries the sense of ‘fall/ending’ and the whole segment states that the present story has concluded. The verb preceding the noun is a poetic word order.
segments.s206-g88: کنون = “now”; رزم کاموس = “the combat/battle of Kamus” (کاموس here is a personal name — it is not the common nouns listed in the senses and is therefore left unspecified); باید is the impersonal modal “it must / one must”; سرود is used as a noun “song/ode” (i.e. “must be sung/recited”). The clause says that the next subject to be recited is the battle of Kamus.
segments.s206-g73: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (a verbal noun/command 'watch, keep') + کن (imperative 'do'), so together 'keep watch' or 'be on guard.' خردمند کارآگهان are two adjectives/nouns in apposition used as a vocative address ('wise, those aware of affairs'), modifying the addressee of the imperative.
segments.s206-g74: This is a continuation of the command: بهرجای is a locative expression 'everywhere / to every place,' followed by the imperative verb بفرست 'send!'. گرد جهان is an adverbial phrase 'around the world' (گرد = 'around/about', جهان = 'world') giving the scope of the sending.
segments.s206-g75: که introduces the content clause 'that.' کیخسرو is the subject (the king). امروز is a temporal adverb 'today.' با is the preposition 'with/by,' and خواستست is the older/periphrastic form (خواست + است) meaning 'it is his will / he has willed' — here indicating that the following actions are done by his will.
segments.s206-g76: بداد و دهش are two coordinated nouns indicating what follows (literally 'badād and giving/grant'); they function as the content that 'adorns' the world. آراستست is formed from آراست (past/participle of 'adorn') + است (copula) meaning 'is adorned' or 'has adorned' — the clause states the world is (made) adorned by those things.
segments.s206-g77: A paratactic list of honors (نژاد 'lineage', بزرگی 'greatness', تخت 'throne', کلاه 'crown/hat') joined by و. They form the topic/accumulation referred to in the following clause.
segments.s206-g78: چو شد is a temporal clause 'when (it) became' (چو = when; شد = became). گرد here functions adverbially ('complete/round/about'), and ازین is the combination 'از این' ('from this'); بیش چیزی مخواه is a negative imperative 'ask for nothing more' (بیش = more; چیزی = anything/something; مخواه = do not desire/ask). Overall: 'When it is all thus, seek nothing more.'
segments.s206-g79: ز is the poetic form of از 'from.' برگشتن (verbal noun) 'the returning' + دشمن 'of the enemy' forms a genitive/infinitival phrase 'the enemy's return.' ایمن مشو is a negative imperative: ایمن ('safe') + مشو ('do not become') = 'do not be secure.'
segments.s206-g80: زمان تا زمان is an idiomatic repetition meaning 'from time to time' (تا linking the two instances). آگهی خواه نو: آگهی 'news/information' with خواه used as a verbal demand/imperative 'seek/ask for' and نو 'anew/fresh' — together: 'keep asking for news afresh.'

sentences.s206-s25: They hung all the garments as ornaments, and they poured dirhams upon his head.
sentences.s206-s26: When he came near the king's city, the spahbed received him with an army.
sentences.s206-s27: He exclaimed 'Bravo!' many times and said that among the champions you have no equal.
sentences.s206-s28: For two weeks, from Afrasiab's iwan the sound of the harp and rebab kept rising.
sentences.s206-s21: Better that we linger with the cup, and let us spend the days one by one.
sentences.s206-s22: When that news reached Afrasiab, he let loose a monster at the time of sleep.
sentences.s206-s23: The spahbed became glad at that news, and he was freed from care and heartache.
sentences.s206-s24: His whole army grew cheerful; they held/their set up the hero's ceremonial procession.
sentences.s206-s13: The vanguard came forward before the army and saw no one in that place.
sentences.s206-s14: He quickly sent word to the elders that the world had become empty of Iranians.
sentences.s206-s15: When the elders also heard (this), they at once sent messengers to every side.
sentences.s206-s16: When the leaders' return became certain, the spahbed washed the soul of sorrow away.
sentences.s206-s5: It is surely necessary to fall upon the king; let us see what the time will turn to.
sentences.s206-s6: If the king's heart is not full of war, there is no place for you and me to take aim.
sentences.s206-s7: A son became fatherless and a father became sonless; some were slain, and the living were left with weary hearts.
sentences.s206-s8: If the ruler gives the command for war, he will raise a renowned army.
sentences.s206-s1: When the shining one lifted its head from the mountain, the crown of the white day arose.
sentences.s206-s2: The scattered host gathered together; each man went on telling tales.
sentences.s206-s3: for many of the Iranians were slain; the proud commander returned.
sentences.s206-s4: Thus the Turks' hand prevailed in battle; now there is no place for delay for the army.
sentences.s206-s17: He himself came with the army to spend the night, and kept moving about that battlefield.
sentences.s206-s18: All the mountains, the plains, the marshes and the rāgh — every pavilion and tent — were like a garden.
sentences.s206-s19: He dismissed the host and withdrew himself, left in wonder at the affairs of the world.
sentences.s206-s20: For one day there is exaltation and another day descent; sometimes it brings joy, sometimes a rebuke.
sentences.s206-s9: Let us come and fill hearts with hatred and war; let us make this world narrow for the ill‑minded.
sentences.s206-s10: On that counsel they turned back to the frontier; all hearts were full of blood and souls full of burning anguish.
sentences.s206-s11: Brothers, stained by brothers' blood, were stricken with grief; their tongues, because of kin, were filled with remembrance.
sentences.s206-s12: They all went at once toward the river‑bowl; their souls, because of those slain, were full of prayers/blessings.
sentences.s206-s29: In the week (period) the elders so counseled that the place would be restored with rejoicing.
sentences.s206-s30: Afrasiab arrayed a certain robe of honour, so that when people began to count there would be a rush.
sentences.s206-s31: Of dinars and regal gems; of golden girdles jeweled and adorned.
sentences.s206-s32: From Arabian steeds much praised; from Indian swords great scabbards.
sentences.s206-s33: One richly appointed throne of ivory and saj, a cradle of turquoise and a crown from off the beaten path.
sentences.s206-s34: A Chinese attendant and a Roman slave; two turquoise cups full of musk and amber.
sentences.s206-s35: He sent things to the nearby elders; thereafter he also gave many counsels.
sentences.s206-s36: ‘Be with the mōbeds and be alert; guard the army from the enemy.’
sentences.s206-s41: To the place where Rostam was — the champion — you, safe, turned your course.
sentences.s206-s42: All the elders accepted his counsel, for he was their leader and their bond.
sentences.s206-s43: The commander of the elders and that assembly set their heads toward the road to Khotan.
sentences.s206-s44: Thus this tale came to an end; now the battle of Kamus must be sung.
sentences.s206-s37: Keep watch, O wise and alert ones; send (men) everywhere about the world.
sentences.s206-s38: For Kay Khosrow today, by his will, has adorned the world with giving and gifts.
sentences.s206-s39: When lineage and greatness and throne and crown have been completed from this, ask for nothing more.
sentences.s206-s40: Do not feel secure against the enemy's return; from time to time seek news anew.
